text: What a day in the market! Oil prices and bond yields finally cooled down, and $NVDA earnings anticipation became the major reason behind today’s rally. All major indices closed up more than 1%, with strong buying across AI-related sectors, semiconductors, photonics, and AI infrastructure plays. After days of selling pressure, today looked like confidence returning back into growth and tech names.

text:
Assume 100mw orbital cluster.
w/o photonics GPUs draw 80mw, networking/interconnect 15mw, other systems 5mw, say 55% gpu util, useful compute = 80mw * 55%=44mw

photonics: networking goes from 15mw -> 6mw, ssaved 9mw goes to compute + better bandwidth raises util from 55% to 70%

Photonics help in 4 main ways IMO: 1) higher bandwidth density (i.e. more Tbps per chip/package/fiber), 2) lower pJ/bit, 3) longer reach than copper esp important for satellite and orbital nodes, 4) higher radix and flatter networks

text:
I've been seeing a lot of people online be bullish on copper and energy companies due to the AI demand. However I'm looking at photonics and microLED companies instead. MicroLED optical interconnects replace traditional copper wires and expensive laser systems by repurposing microLED display pixels as high-speed optical transceivers.  If they are able to use photonics instead, it will greatly diminish the demand for copper and energy.   

Some of you may remember me talking about QCLS a few months ago; it is a similar tech. I passed on it as a long hold though because the fundamentals looked a bit too high risk for me. $FABC  on the other hand has $30m in cash and plans to demonstrate the tech before the end of the year. They also have 2 NDA's signed with industry leading chip makers. Also KOPN has partnered with them on this. This isn't striking me as a typical penny stock.  

Also, if you look at the chart on FABC it is down quite a bit from the high. However FABC started trading under $FABC (https://t.co/whdtkxDNsi) in late April 2026. It's essentially a new company but the chart is deceptive. It used to be a completely different company.  

Anyway, I don't think KOPN would've partnered with them if they weren't onto something. I've been looking to gain exposure on this tech for a while and $POET, $KOPN and $FABC  are the three that I think will benefit from it.

If they are able to use photonics instead, it will greatly diminish the demand for copper and energy. Some of you may remember me talking about QCLS a few months ago; it is a similar tech. I passed on it as a long hold though because the fundamentals looked a bit too high risk for me. $FABC on the other hand has $30m in cash and plans to demonstrate the tech before the end of the year. They also have 2 NDA's signed with industry leading chip makers. Also KOPN has partnered with them on this. This isn't striking me as a typical penny stock. Also, if you look at the chart on FABC it is down quite a bit from the high. However FABC started trading under $FABC (https://t.co/whdtkxDNsi) in late April 2026. It's essentially a new company but the chart is deceptive. It used to be a completely different company. Anyway, I don't think KOPN would've partnered with them if they weren't onto something. I've been looking to gain exposure on this tech for a while and $POET, $KOPN and $FABC are the three that I think will benefit from it."}}}, "rest_id": "2057214805816270976", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "176", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
AI clusters face hard limits on how many accelerators you can connect before power and latency kill the gains. Lightmatter is bringing 3D photonics and THz optical fabrics out of the lab and into real data center designs.

This changes the game for scaling. Optical I/O slashes energy per bit moved across racks and enables denser, more efficient AI factories. Next-gen models could train faster and cheaper as interconnect bottlenecks ease, putting pressure on traditional electrical networking suppliers while opening doors for photonic specialists.

The asymmetric setup most investors will recognize too late:👀

$SIVE sits at ~$2B mcap today.

The InP chokepoint of AI photonics. Four AI infrastructure pillars. One company.

The CPO market alone is projected at $20B by 2036 (37% CAGR).

If $SIVE captures just 5% of the External Light Source sub-market:
→ $1B revenue
→ At 10-15x premium photonics multiple
→ $10-15B mcap
→ 5-7x from here

That doesn't even count:
✚ LiDAR ramp (Aeva/NVIDIA Hyperion Q4 2026)
✚ 5G/6G mmWave commercialization (Tachyon)
✚ Defense program expansion (NEMC EW STAR)
✚ Jabil custom transceiver scale-up
✚ Nasdaq New York dual listing unlock
✚ U.S.-Sweden TPD bilateral cooperation tailwinds

When $LITE and $COHR re-rated on NVIDIA's $2B investment, the picks-and-shovels thesis was validated.

$SIVE is the smallest, most concentrated InP pure-play of the three Western suppliers.

The market hasn't priced this yet.🤯

When it does, the entry isn't here anymore.🚀

Long $SIVE $SIVEF

text:
$SIVE - The InP supply chain reality most AI investors miss:

Silicon photonics handles routing & modulation.
It CANNOT produce light.

Every AI optical interconnect, every co-packaged optics (CPO) system, every silicon photonic data center stack needs an EXTERNAL Indium Phosphide (InP) laser as the light source.

→ Sub-30% InP wafer yields industry-wide
→ Only 3 scaled Western suppliers: IQE (UK), Coherent (US), Sivers (Sweden)
→ Demand running ahead of available production
→ Sivers is the most concentrated InP pure-play of the three

This is the picks-and-shovels chokepoint in AI photonics.🔥

text: The big issue for Nvidia is what happens after Feynman+. They have reached the literal limits of physics after 0.5nm. There is nothing there. text:
Both are AIM-listed deep tech plays riding the same photonics and AI wave.
But they are different animals in my view. 
$SIVE is a chip designer and manufacturer. Revenue already at scale. Moving fast.
$ENSI is an ASIC design house. The revenue ramp is earlier stage, tied to chips converting from design to supply.
Could ENSI follow SIVE's trajectory? The pipeline suggests yes. The timeline is longer.
If you have patience, the setup is arguably more asymmetric.

text:
MssCorp (https://t.co/MMqfwsnwyH)  2026/5/27 earnings presentation leak :

-Self-developed MSS HG (Helmet Gecko) Silicon Photonics tester:

• Three HG units have been assembled and are now operational, providing silicon photonics R&D analysis and engineering validation services to multiple clients.
• The core optical loss detection technology has received patents in Taiwan, Japan, and the United States.
• The system has successfully completed certification by major US AI chip manufacturers.
• PD and QA production versions are scheduled for launch in 2026.
• High-volume commercial ramp-up is expected in 2027.
This development represents one of the company’s four key growth engines, advancing the transition from analysis services to a higher-margin business model encompassing equipment sales, IP licensing, and integrated measurement services in the expanding CPO/silicon photonics market.

Full presentation deck: https://t.co/RVLtv96uSD

embedded_text:
@th3Oneorac3d Active alignment is slow - it can take minutes per unit, which kills your throughput. POET's passive approach runs at standard semiconductor speeds, so you're talking seconds instead of minutes. That's huge for volume production.

POET's passive approach runs at standard semiconductor speeds, so you're talking seconds instead of minutes. That's huge for volume production. Roughly 2 orders of magnitude (100x) improvement per device in effective alignment/assembly time. ￼ Why ~2 orders of magnitude? • Traditional active alignment (most photonics competitors): Requires powering up the device + real-time feedback loops to optimize coupling. This is typically done serially (one module at a time) and takes tens of seconds to several minutes per device/module. ￼ • POET passive alignment: Mechanically self-aligning via pre-etched features on the wafer. Uses standard semiconductor pick-and-place tools — ~0.1–few seconds per component placement, done in a highly parallel wafer-level process (500+ engines per 8” wafer)."}}}, "rest_id": "2058429967827079439", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2229", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2059055899705332000", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "106", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Feels like the market is hunting for anything tied to the AI infrastructure and photonics buildout right now, especially smaller high beta names.

Interesting seeing capital rotate further down the stack too. $XMAX has started popping up more for a similar reason with the AI infrastructure narrative plus the indirect SpaceX and xAI exposure layered on top.

$LPTH deep dive:

LightPath is a real little defense/edge-optics story, not just another “AI photonics” ticker someone found under a couch cushion.

Q3 FY26 revenue was USD 19.1M, +109% YoY. Gross profit was USD 7.0M, +161%. Gross margin hit 36%, adjusted EBITDA was positive at USD 1.1M, and backlog reached roughly USD 110.6M, up 196% from June 2025.

The thesis: BlackDiamond germanium-free infrared optics, G5 imaging systems, and U.S. optical manufacturing could become useful in counter-drone, ISR, border, weapons, and autonomous sensing programs. This is physical AI / defense-sensor plumbing, not data-center CPO.

The data is good enough to care. TTM revenue is only about USD 62.8M, but backlog is already more than 1.7x TTM sales. Management is targeting more than USD 300M of revenue within five years. Consensus has FY26 revenue around USD 72.7M and FY27 around USD 101.3M, so estimate revisions are the actual battleground.

Top catalysts I care about:

1. Q4/FY27 guide proving backlog converts into revenue and margin.
2. A named defense program or prime/customer order above USD 20M.
3. BlackDiamond / second U.S. glass capacity turning from slide-deck promise into qualified throughput and orders.

Risks: the stock already ripped to USD 16.95 after a +18.7% day, above the USD 15.98 average sell-side target and basically at the USD 17 high target. GAAP losses and negative FCF still matter. Defense procurement can turn “pipeline” into “please wait 18 months.” And the social signal is still mostly single-source.

Entry/Verdict: CATALYST WATCH / no chase. Optional 0.25%-0.50% scout only if you want defense-optics optionality. Add-size only after backlog conversion, >35% gross margin, positive adjusted EBITDA, or a named USD 20M+ defense order.

I like the proof. I hate paying victory-lap prices before the next contract shows up. We'll see how it goes.

text:
Here’s my long-winded post on $SIVE for the weekend.

Next week is bloody important. ER hits, MSCI inclusion soon after. 

$SIVE Catalysts:

Sivers Semiconductors is a Swedish leader in photonics (high-power lasers for AI data centers/co-packaged optics) and wireless/mmWave tech (5G/6G, SATCOM, beamformers). 

The company is riding multiple tailwinds in AI infrastructure, defense, and transatlantic tech cooperation.

Key Recent & Upcoming Catalysts:

1. CHIPS Act Funding (US Strategic Validation)
•  Sivers has secured awards through the Northeast Microelectronics Coalition (NEMC) Hub under the US CHIPS and Science Act (Microelectronics Commons program).
•  Awards include ~$6M+ initial funding for 5G/6G beamformer ICs/array design (FR3 frequencies) and Electronic Warfare projects, with potential to reach ~$11.6M+ in year 1 and up to ~$30M over multiple years if extended. https://t.co/tNTC2ClWf8 
•  Partners include major players like Ericsson, Raytheon, BAE Systems, MIT, and others. This de-risks development, funds US-aligned prototyping, and strengthens Sivers’ position in secure supply chains for defense and advanced comms.

2. White House / US-Sweden Technology Prosperity Deal
•  Recent signing of the US-Sweden Technology Prosperity Deal (highlighted in White House releases and statements by Secretary of State Rubio) deepens cooperation in AI, 5G/6G, biotech, quantum, defense tech, advanced manufacturing, and trusted infrastructure. 
•  This bilateral push aligns perfectly with Sivers’ portfolio in AI optics, lasers, photonics, and wireless tech. It signals stronger US-Sweden tech ties, potential for more collaboration/funding, and elevated strategic importance for Swedish innovators like Sivers. 

3. Nasdaq Dual Listing Evaluation
•  In April 2026, Sivers announced it’s evaluating a dual listing on Nasdaq New York (while keeping Swedish domicile). This aims to tap deeper US capital markets, broaden the investor base, and support growth in AI/photonics. https://t.co/tNTC2ClWf8 
•  Preparations include audit uplifts to US standards (PCAOB). Positive for liquidity, visibility, and valuation re-rating as US investors gain easier access.

4. Other Strong Tailwinds:
•  AI Optics Boom: High-power lasers for co-packaged optics in next-gen AI data centers. Partnerships (e.g., O-Net, POET) targeting volume production in late 2026. Strong demand with structural shortages noted by peers. @i 
•  Pipeline growth: ~$453M (up 64% YoY), product revenue mix improving significantly. @hoarderresearch 
•  Q1 results expected May 29, 2026 – potential positive print catalyst.
•  Index inclusions and overall sector momentum in AI/semiconductors.
$SIVE has seen sharp moves recently on AI/photonics hype, but remains a smaller-cap story with real execution and policy support behind it. The combination of CHIPS funding, US-Sweden tech pact, Nasdaq ambitions, and AI optics exposure creates a compelling multi-catalyst setup.
Not financial advice – DYOR. Markets are volatile, especially in small-cap tech/semiconductors.

What are your thoughts on $SIVE? 

Holding or watching? 🚀

text:
Then comes the real late-cycle signal: productization.

$DRAM, $EUV, $LYTE and other narrow AI-infrastructure ETFs for memory, photonics, etc. are now showing up. Leveraged versions and filings are following too.

First the stocks run.
Then the ETF wrappers come.
Then leveraged retail products arrive.

Historically, that is not early-cycle behavior. This is late-cycle behavior. 

9/12

⚖️ Embedded Photonics in Vehicles — Science vs. Speculation

Let’s separate what is scientifically plausible from what remains unproven.

Embedded photonics in vehicles is not science fiction.

Modern vehicles already contain:
• LiDAR systems
• infrared sensing
• radar arrays
• optical cameras
• laser-based range finding
• fiber-optic communications
• HUD projection systems
• adaptive lighting systems
• driver monitoring cameras
• occupancy sensors
• gesture recognition
• and autonomous navigation architectures

These are all forms of applied photonics:
the science of generating, transmitting, detecting, or manipulating light.

🧠 Why Automakers Use Embedded Photonics:

Legitimate applications include:
• collision avoidance
• autonomous driving
• lane detection
• facial/eye tracking for safety
• interior occupancy mapping
• adaptive headlights
• augmented reality HUD overlays
• V2X communication systems
• and environmental sensing

⚖️ Witness Science Observation:

As vehicles become:
• connected
• sensor-dense
• AI-assisted
• and continuously networked

they increasingly function less like mechanical machines and more like:
mobile sensor platforms.

That shift matters.

Especially when:
• telemetry
• behavioral analytics
• geolocation
• and environmental mapping

become economically valuable.

🧠 Important Scientific Boundary:

The existence of embedded photonics in vehicles does NOT prove:
• targeting systems
• weaponization
• behavioral manipulation
• or covert surveillance operations

However:
the underlying sensing architecture itself is real, documented, and expanding rapidly across the automotive industry.

⚖️ What Investigators Should Scientifically Examine:

If anomalous vehicle behavior is alleged, investigators should review:
• telemetry logs
• sensor synchronization
• LiDAR/radar outputs
• occupancy sensor anomalies
• network traffic
• CAN bus activity
• wireless module communications
• HUD/projector systems
• camera calibration records
• and firmware integrity

Because in modern vehicles:
light itself is part of the operating system.

text:
$SIVE CEO confirming major brands are in active talks for co-packaged optics and pluggables is the kind of validation that turns a small photonics name into a real structural winner.

this isn’t vague hope; it’s the CEO on record saying the pipeline is converting from design wins into serious commercial discussions. momentum is clearly building.

i’m biased that this is exactly where the highest asymmetry sits right now. hyperscalers scaling to 800G and 1.6T+ are hitting hard limits on interconnect density, heat, and power that copper can’t fix. sivers’ high-performance CW lasers are a critical enabling technology for both pluggables today and CPO tomorrow.

the recent CHIPS Act grant with BAE and MIT Lincoln Lab plus the customer mapping (Ayar Labs, POET, Marvell Celestial, Jabil) give it real credibility and non-dilutive backing. this is the type of quiet accumulation that precedes bigger volume orders.

photonics is one of those constraints, and $SIVE sits right in the middle of the laser chokepoint.

text:
$ELMT $TSM $NVDA $MU The Elmet Group serves as a critical upstream supplier to the semiconductor fabrication ecosystem.

🔸 The most important ELMT product categories supporting the production of NVIDIA-class AI chips, advanced memory (like HBM and NAND), and silicon photonics include:

——————————-
🔸 1. PVD (Physical Vapor Deposition): This is a major one.

• Needed heavily in:

• advanced packaging,
• HBM integration,
• GPU interconnect layers,
• transistor stack formation.

• directly connects to advanced-node GPU fabrication.

——————————-
🔸 2. CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition) Critical for:

• transistor layers,
• dielectric films,
• advanced node structures,
• nanosheet/GAAFET processes,
• insulating films.

• NVIDIA-class chips absolutely rely on advanced CVD steps.

———————————
🔸 3. MBE (Molecular Beam Epitaxy) important for:

• compound semiconductors,
• photonics,
• advanced RF devices,
• optical interconnects.

• silicon photonics,
• co-packaged optics,
• optical networking,
• advanced interconnects

———————————-
🔸 4. Thermal Management
Very important for AI chips: 

• Especially:

• H100,
• B200,
• Rubin-class future chips,
• HBM stacks,
• AI accelerators.

———————————
🔸 5. Crystal Growth
Critical upstream process: 

• Used for:
• wafer production,
• sapphire growth,
• semiconductor substrate creation.

• These are foundational to semiconductor material manufacturing.

——————————-
🔸 6. ELMT supplies the infrastructure/material stack behind:

• semiconductor fabrication tools,
• deposition systems,
• ion implantation systems,
• thermal environments.

ELMT is a materials + survivability infrastructure inside semiconductor manufacturing equipment. 

———————————
🔸 7. Semiconductor Equipment Makers, Photonics, Aerospace Defense:

text:
Nvidia earnings drop today at 5pm EST.

Jensen has already told us what to expect:

Every piece of the supply chain is locked in. The HBM. The Grace Blackwells. The CPUs. The COOSs. The silicon photonics. All of it coordinated, all of it aligned.

His exact words: "The demand is much greater than the overall capacity."

That's a green light.

Think about what that actually means. Nvidia has the largest supply chain in the world. Their partners have spent years securing allocation. The hardest part of building a chip at this scale is getting all the pieces to come together at the same time.

And demand still outstrips all of it.

This isn't a story about Nvidia struggling to build. It's a story about the world not being able to get enough of what Nvidia builds. Every hyperscaler, every sovereign AI fund, every startup racing to train the next model are all in line.

Nvidia isn't constrained by its own execution. It's constrained by how fast the world can absorb what it builds.

That's the best problem a company can have going into an earnings call.

text:
Corning $GLW just introduced a new Photonics platform they plan to scale into a $10 billion annual revenue stream by 2030.

They're targeting:

- $20 billion annualized sales by end of 2026
- $27 billion by end of 2028 (with an internal goal of $30 billion)
- And ~$35 billion by year-end 2030 (with an aspiration of $40 billion)

That's a big roadmap for a stock that the market still treats like a sleepy little glass company.

One in... glass? His take (save this): Most of $AMZN's 2026 run is already in the rearview. He's still bullish long term, but the easy money got made. So he's rotating that capital into higher conviction setups. The two names he picked: Micron $MU and Corning $GLW. Here's why… 1/ Micron $MU. The market is fixated on the wrong story here - everyone's panicking over China after reports that Nvidia's planned AI chip sales there never materialized. Traders read that as 'softer Chinese AI demand' and dumped memory stocks. But that totally misses what's happening on the supply side... Samsung is in the middle of a strike that could knock 3-4% of global DRAM supply offline. In a market this tight, that's MASSIVE. Those orders then shift to whoever can fill them. And Micron is at the front of that line. On top of that, Micron's HBM3E and HBM4 capacity is fully sold out through 2026. Hyperscalers and model builders are scrambling for high bandwidth memory because GPUs are useless without it. And the stock already pulled back hard. It's now trading at a forward PE under 8 while revenue is expected to grow over 100% next year. Yes, it'll be volatile. But in Melvin's view, the market is somehow STILL underpricing how central memory is in the AI stack. That's $MU. Then there's... 2/ Corning $GLW. Another bottleneck in AI outside of memory, is everything around the GPU. (Fiber, optics, glass, connectivity, power) I.e. the plumbing that lets data centers actually move data between servers and clusters at the speeds AI workloads demand. Corning sits directly in that lane. They make the optical fiber, connectivity hardware, and glass infrastructure that AI data centers run on - and optical cable lead times are reportedly getting stretched as demand overwhelms supply. Corning is expanding U.S. optical connectivity manufacturing capacity by 10x, increasing U.S. fiber production by more than 50%, and building three new plants to support AI factory buildouts. The numbers from Corning's May 2026 investor day back it up: Management introduced a new Photonics platform they plan to scale into a $10 billion annual revenue stream by 2030. They're targeting $20 billion annualized sales by end of 2026, $27 billion by end of 2028 with an internal goal of $30 billion, and around $35 billion by year-end 2030 with an aspiration of $40 billion. That's a big roadmap for a stock that the market still treats like a sleepy little glass company. Want to see exactly how much $AMZN Melvin just sold - and how he sized the $MU and $GLW buys? Pipeline ≠ revenue yet. I’d be watching execution, margins, cash burn, and the insider investigation closely.

text:
A team at Westlake University in China just published a handheld cancer detector in Nature Photonics. Single drop of blood, 94.9% accuracy for early-stage lung cancer, 10,000× more sensitive than the standard lab test.

Chip cost: $5.Worth paying attention to, but also worth reading carefully. That 94.9% comes from 171 patient samples. The device replaces refrigerator-sized lab equipment with an LED, a photodetector, and a 3D metamaterial chip. Genuinely impressive physics.

However, the gap nobody's talking about: every year produces 3-4 "revolutionary cancer detection" papers. Almost none survive the jump from Nature paper to clinical validation at scale.

Caveat here: 171 patients is a starting point, not proof. But the engineering approach is what's worth watching.

text:
The AI bottlenecks that are forming as we speak

$MRVL supplies critical connectivity silicon. $COHR is critical for its industrial-scale photonics manufacturing. Big backing by $NVDA. $LITE is critical for its continuous wave laser components and early high-speed lane adoption. Also backed by $NVDA.

For bandwidth and capacity limits, $ALAB has a dominant position for scaling up AI clusters.

For cooling, $VRT has been the dominate leader for the data center cooling market.

For grid exhaustion, $CEG is the leader in dedicated nuclear generation and is the largest pure-play carbon-free nuclear operator. $VST has decade-long agreements with hyperscalers too for nuclear power. $OKLO is also a strong contender for dedicated nuclear generation.

$AVGO is the critical enabler of the ASIC revolution. They are engineering custom silicon to minimize power and interconnect penalties. 

$CBRS is proposing a way to bypass the interconnect wall by utilizing wafer-scale alternatives. This bypasses the need for co-packaged optics by manufacturing AI chips as a single, massive silicon wafers, which keeps the pathways fully on chip.

text:
Çinli araştırmacılar, bir damla kanla akciğer kanserini erken evrede tespit edebilen avuç içi boyutunda bir cihaz geliştirdi.

Cihaz, geleneksel ekipmanlara göre 10.000 kat daha hassas ve yüzde 94,9 doğruluk oranına sahip. Çalışma, Nature Photonics dergisinde yayımlandı.

#ÇinliAraştırmacılar #AkciğerKanseri

text:
THE PHOTONICS LAYER OF THE AI BUILDOUT

Goldman Sachs says the AI optical networking market growing nearly 10x to $150B+ by 2028 as clusters scale into multi-rack systems like $NVDA Rubin Ultra NVL576.

The AI bottleneck is shifting to the fabric that lets hundreds of GPUs behave like one system driving a massive dollar content expansion across NVLink, co-packaged optics & optical interconnects: 

• $AEHR burn-in & reliability test systems for photonics and AI hardware
• $AAOI data center optical transceivers with in-house laser manufacturing
• $LITE high-speed lasers & optical components for data center transceivers
• $VIAV optical testing & measurement tools used to validate high-speed networks
• $COHR lasers, photonic components & materials used across optical networking systems
• $MRVL optical DSPs, custom interconnect silicon & networking chips for AI infrastructure
• $CRDO retimers, DSPs & active electrical cables for rack-level signal integrity in AI clusters https://t.co/70aHTSwMZa

$AVGO:
- Currently on 3rd generation of CPO-enabled switch ASIC w/ the TH6 Davisson released in H2'25

$MRVL: 
- Competes in CPO for both its XPU + switch ASIC businesses.
- Celestial acquisition gives stronger LT foothold in CPO; $AMZN is the first hyperscaler design-win for their PF chiplets.

$LITE: 
- In a great position to take advantage of the CPO inflection given its capabilities in optical components, In-house InP production.
- Multi-yr supply agreement w/ Nvidia in exchange for $2B equity is in the multibillion dollar sales range through 2030.

$COHR:
- Expects a $5B TAM for CPO.
- Received $2B equity from Nvidia in exchange for a multi-billion CPO supply agreement starting CY27 + going through CY30 (lasers, modules, etc.).

$BESI (BE Semiconductor):
- Leader in hybrid bonding + is exposed to CPO via its advanced die-attach and packaging solutions which are critical for integrating photonic chips w/ switch ASICs in CPO architectures.
- Besi noted that Nvidia + Broadcom switches use multiple hybrid bonding processes.

$AIXA (Aixtron):
- 90% share in epi equipment for optoelectronics (lasers + SiPh devices) for high-speed data communication.

$SOI (Soitec):
- Integrates photonic components on a silicon-on-insulator (SOI) + provides significant benefits for optical transceivers.
- E.g. faster data rates, cost efficiency, power savings, smaller size + better signal integrity.

Note: the above are direct summaries/quotes from BofA's report.

Incredibly... 

that is ~146% of FY2025 revenue. https://t.co/ahQWyxgrMh

text:
let's reason from first principles on what this list is actually saying.

$AXTI up 761%. #SNDK up 523%. #AAOI up 421%. #AEHR up 371%. #NVTS up 310%.

this is not a meme list. this is a semiconductor materials and photonics sweep. every single name in the top 10 touches either compound semiconductors, optical interconnects, flash storage, or power electronics.

the market spent 2024 and 2025 arguing about which hyperscaler wins the ai race. meanwhile the actual winners were the people selling the shovels to build the picks to mine the gold.

#BE at 248%. #DOCN at 229%. #INTC at 225%. #MU at 163%.

even intel. INTEL. the company everyone wrote the obituary for. up 225%.

you'd have to actively avoid the data to have missed the infrastructure layer thesis. grok flagged compound semiconductors and optical interconnect as the underloved layer of the ai stack 18 months ago. the consensus was chasing software multiples.

the physics was always simple: you cannot move data at ai speeds without photonics. you cannot train at ai scale without memory bandwidth. #MU and #LITE were not mysteries. they were thermodynamics.

the analysts who were bearish on semis in early 2025 are very quiet right now. concerningly quiet.

text:
$SIVE

The White House just put US-Sweden cooperation around AI, 5G/6G, cloud, electronics, defense tech, advanced manufacturing and trusted infrastructure on paper.

That matters.

Because this is no longer just a “small Swedish photonics story.”

This is starting to become a strategic infrastructure story.

Sivers is exposed to the exact layers the US and Sweden now want to deepen:

AI data center optics
laser sources
advanced connectivity
defense-grade photonics
trusted tech supply chains
US capital market optionality

The market may still see $SIVE as a tiny Nordic semiconductor name.

I think that is the opportunity.

Photonic chokepoints are moving from niche to national strategy.

text:
$SIVE Sivers Semiconductors: Strategic Validation Across AI Photonics, RF and Defense Infrastructure

https://t.co/EnmkiWUSEN

This $6.6M Year 2 EW STAR award is a very important signal, in my view.

The contract value itself is not the main point. The main point is continued validation from within the U.S. defense microelectronics ecosystem.

Sivers is working alongside BAE Systems, MIT Lincoln Laboratory and Columbia University on next-generation electronic warfare, radar and advanced communications architectures. For a small-cap semiconductor company, that is a serious credibility marker.

What I like most is how the Sivers story is broadening.

This is not only AI/datacenter photonics.

It is AI photonics plus InP lasers, SATCOM/LEO connectivity, advanced RF, beamforming, defense communications and potential U.S. institutional access.

That combination can become extremely powerful if execution continues.

Many semiconductor companies have one promising vertical. Sivers appears to be building relevance across several strategic markets at the same time — each tied to bandwidth, latency, power efficiency, secure connectivity and national security.

That is why I believe the market may still be far from fully pricing in the strategic value of this company.

The award is not just funding.

It is validation that Sivers is being pulled deeper into critical infrastructure layers where specialized semiconductor technology matters.

The contract value itself is not the main point. The main point is continued validation from within the U.S. defense microelectronics ecosystem. Sivers is working alongside BAE Systems, MIT Lincoln Laboratory and Columbia University on next-generation electronic warfare, radar and advanced communications architectures. For a small-cap semiconductor company, that is a serious credibility marker. What I like most is how the Sivers story is broadening. This is not only AI/datacenter photonics. It is AI photonics plus InP lasers, SATCOM/LEO connectivity, advanced RF, beamforming, defense communications and potential U.S. institutional access. That combination can become extremely powerful if execution continues. Many semiconductor companies have one promising vertical. Sivers appears to be building relevance across several strategic markets at the same time — each tied to bandwidth, latency, power efficiency, secure connectivity and national security. That is why I believe the market may still be far from fully pricing in the strategic value of this company. The award is not just funding. It is validation that Sivers is being pulled deeper into critical infrastructure layers where specialized semiconductor technology matters."}}}, "rest_id": "2057905440852209928", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2427", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

But the real shocker in this Morgan Stanley slide is actually power, because the industry is now talking about moving from roughly 120kW per rack today toward potentially 600kW per rack by the Vera Rubin Ultra generation in 2027, which is an almost unimaginable escalation in power density within an incredibly short period of time.

To put this into perspective, many traditional enterprise datacenters historically operated at only a few kilowatts per rack, while even modern hyperscale campuses today often consume only tens of megawatts in total facility power draw. But once you begin deploying hundreds or thousands of 600kW AI racks simultaneously, the math becomes almost absurd because a large-scale Vera Rubin Ultra cluster could eventually consume gigawatts of electricity, effectively rivaling the energy demand of a mid-sized city.

And this is where the market still massively underestimates the second-order implications of the AI boom, because the bottleneck is no longer simply semiconductors, GPUs, or memory supply. The bottleneck increasingly becomes electricity itself.

The US power grid can barely keep up with current AI infrastructure demand already, while transmission congestion, transformer shortages, substation constraints, cooling limitations, permitting bottlenecks, and aging grid infrastructure are becoming increasingly visible across major datacenter hubs. Importantly, grid infrastructure cannot scale at semiconductor speed. You can accelerate chip production with enough capital expenditure and engineering talent, but building transmission lines, substations, generation capacity, cooling systems, and interconnection approvals often requires many years due to environmental reviews, local opposition, labor shortages, and physical construction constraints.

This is precisely why we continue believing the AI buildout is not a two-to-three-year investment cycle, but instead a decade-long industrial transformation that increasingly resembles the buildout of railroads, electricity networks, and telecom infrastructure during previous industrial revolutions.

And this is also why energy infrastructure is quietly becoming one of the most important and underappreciated AI trades globally.

The winners are no longer just GPU companies. The winners increasingly include utilities like Constellation Energy and Vistra, nuclear-related plays like Oklo and NuScale Power, gas infrastructure companies like Kinder Morgan and Williams Companies, grid and electrical equipment suppliers like GE Vernova, Eaton, Schneider Electric, and Vertiv, as well as transformer, cooling, and datacenter infrastructure providers that now sit directly inside the physical backbone required to support next-generation compute.

Hyperscalers themselves are starting to understand this reality. Companies like Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta are no longer simply software companies buying servers. They are increasingly becoming quasi-energy infrastructure companies because securing long-duration power availability is becoming strategically inseparable from securing compute capacity itself.

That is why nuclear power is quietly returning to the center of the conversation. Hyperscalers may eventually fund or directly partner on nuclear generation projects out of pure necessity because renewable intermittency alone cannot reliably support ultra-high-density AI clusters operating continuously at scale.

In many ways, AI is beginning to collide with physical reality. You cannot run trillion-dollar next-generation compute infrastructure on transmission systems and grid architectures that were largely built decades ago for a completely different industrial era.

The semiconductor story may have started the AI race, but energy infrastructure may ultimately determine who wins it."}}}, "rest_id": "2057723120103170197", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/iphone" rel="nofollow">Twitter for iPhone</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "223124", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2057899801945973187", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/iphone" rel="nofollow">Twitter for iPhone</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1073", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
INTERVIEW ON CPO: Radha Nagarajan, the Senior Vice President and Chief Technology Officer of Marvell’s Optical Engineering Group. 

Q: At what point do you think silicon photonics will take over discrete components/modules?

I would say silicon photonics has already taken over to a large extent, especially at 100 G and higher data rates. Yield is not something unique to just silicon photonics—it is a critical requirement for any practical technology. However, what silicon photonics uniquely enables is particularly important for the industry. First, it allows companies without their own foundry, like Marvell, to design and manufacture highly complex photonic components by leveraging the existing semiconductor infrastructure. Second, it provides true scalability. Marvell, for example, has been one of the well-known companies implementing silicon photonics for applications between datacenters. Intel pioneered silicon photonics for applications inside the datacenters. Today, many of the large transceiver companies have mostly transitioned to silicon photonics. Given this level of adoption, it is fair to say that silicon photonics has already ‘taken over’ in many key segments of the optical interconnect market.

Q: Many silicon photonics systems still rely on external laser packaging. Do you see integrated light sources becoming mainstream, or will off-chip lasers dominate for practical reasons?

If you look at the integrated laser work published over the years, including our early demonstrations, Intel’s results, and developments at Infinera (now Nokia), it is clear that integrating light sources heterogeneously on silicon or monolithically in InP is commercially viable. The real obstacle is not technical capability, but perception—many people still assume that integrated lasers will suffer from reliability issues. One concern stems from the behavior of indium phosphide lasers at elevated temperatures. It is true that performance can degrade at high temperatures, but when operated around a base temperature of roughly 70–75 °C, these lasers can already operate perfectly well. In fact, Intel has demonstrated strong reliability in this regime. The key is careful design. Temperature variations can be significant, and the resulting wavelength shifts must be properly managed. In practice, engineers have already developed effective strategies to handle these challenges. So, from a technical perspective, I do not see major roadblocks. The bigger challenge may be changing people’s mindset.

Let me also make a prediction. Cooling large-scale server infrastructure is becoming increasingly challenging. The industry is already transitioning from air cooling to liquid cooling, and some are exploring immersion cooling. These approaches can provide more stable and potentially lower operating temperatures, which could create an opportunity for integrated lasers as well.

Q: How do you see the potential of heterogeneous integration?

I think silicon photonics, as a platform for integration, has enormous potential. What we’ve achieved so far is only scratching the surface. There are multiple paths forward here. You can integrate different materials, combine multiple types of devices within the same material platform, or even move toward stacking multiple device layers. All of these approaches are actively being explored, and I believe many of them will enter production in the near future. As data rates continue to increase, heterogeneous integration will also involve incorporating new higher performance materials, opening up more possibilities. At the same time, integration itself is becoming more complex. We’re moving beyond traditional 2D integration. People are now talking about 2.5D, 3D, even 3.5D integration, which will create many new opportunities for system design and performance improvement. 

Q: You were one of the early voices pushing co-packaged optics (CPO) for AI systems, now more and more companies are doing this. What convinced you CPO was not just a research direction, but something the industry would truly need?

The reason I have advocated for CPO stems from my firm belief that heterogeneous integration is the key to high-speed applications. During my time at Inphi (now at Marvell), we were already seeing the limits of traditional approaches. As data rates continued to increase, techniques like wire bonds simply could not keep up. The logical next step was to bring electronics and photonics closer through heterogeneous integration. That was the original motivation—overcoming speed and bandwidth bottlenecks. But as we developed these approaches, it became clear that they also align very well with what CPO is trying to achieve. Over time, the value of co-packaging has expanded beyond just pushing the highest data rates. We now see it being applied even at relatively lower data rates, especially in large-scale AI systems. Whether you look at scale-up, scale-out, or scale-across systems, they all benefit from tightly integrated photonics with electronics. So, in a way, heterogeneous integration is the essence, and CPO is one of its most important applications.

Q: Speed is the reason that drives us from ‘pluggable’ to ‘on board’. Many believe ‘pluggable’ can be extended with better DSPs and packaging, while others argue they won’t scale forever’. From your perspective, will this ‘extension strategy’ scale indefinitely? If you had to point to a single limiting factor, what would ultimately block ‘pluggable’ from keeping up with 100 T, or 200 T switch ASICs?

In my opinion, with 100 T or 200 T switches and systems at the horizon, you will need co-packaging. It’s true that the industry is very comfortable with ‘pluggable’ solutions, and there is a strong incentive to extend their life as much as possible. In many scale-up systems, copper will continue to be used as long as it makes sense from a power and cost perspective. For the same reasons, data center operators would naturally prefer to stay with pluggable solutions.

However, when developing new products, you have to look ahead. Since late last year, analysts have begun including CPO in their revenue projections, which signals a real shift in the industry. In fact, data center operators and hyperscalers are now actively funding CPO developments. That tells you this is no longer just a research direction. Technically, pluggables can be pushed further, even beyond 200 G per lane, but the cost is power. As you scale bandwidth, the power required for high-speed electrical interfaces and DSPs becomes increasingly difficult to manage.

This is where CPO becomes very attractive, especially for scale-up architecture like GPU or accelerator interconnects. I actually think the first major deployment for CPO will happen in scale-up systems, and then expand into scale-out.

We’re already seeing strong signals from the ecosystem. Nvidia has demonstrated multiple generations of CPO platforms, including at GTC and ISSCC. From a foundry perspective, this aligns very well with TSMC’s roadmap. The idea of tightly coupling electrical compute with optical I/O is no longer a dream—it could be just a year or two away.

That said, predicting the exact timing is always difficult. It’s much easier to explain the past than to forecast the future. When you work at the intersection of technology development and product deployment, there is always uncertainty about when the market will be ready. In many ways, developing new technologies can feel like placing a bet—and that’s simply part of innovation.

Q: People often say that co-packaging is always the difficult part, what do you think about that?

That perception largely comes from how optical packaging used to be done. If you compare it to electrical packaging, the difference is quite clear. Electrical packaging has been highly automated for a long time. But if you look at the optical packaging in early days, you’ll find yourself doing things one at a time, wire bonding electronics and manually aligning fibers, which are slow and not scalable. But once volume kicks in, things start to change. A lot of these processes are now highly automated. There are still operators involved, but the work is largely handled by automatic equipment. Efficient fiber coupling is still a challenge, no question. But there are already solutions out there, and they’re getting better. As volume continues to ramp, I believe the fiber coupling part will gradually be automated as well. If you look at where we are today, photonics manufacturing is already very different from five years ago, and nothing like ten years ago. And honestly, things are only going to move faster from here.

Q: Are Through-Silicon Via (TSVs) essential for CPO?

We demonstrated and published extensively on TSV as an option way to integrate electronics on top and then route down into silicon photonics bottom layer. But you don’t have to always do that. You can flip the architecture and put the optics on top instead, and some groups are actively exploring that. One reason people hesitate is thermal. Electronics tend to run hotter than optics, so it’s often easier to heat-sink from the top, if the electronics are there. But that’s not a fundamental limitation—there are many alternative thermal management strategies people are exploring as well.

There are also practical considerations in the fab that may lead to a preference for one configuration over another. Early on, there was a strong tendency to adopt TSV-based approach. For example, you now have packaging techniques like fan-out wafer-level packaging (FOWLP), which does not require TSVs at all. Several companies are already offering this technology, and some are even beginning to deploy CPO based on it.

So, the absence of TSVs is not a fundamental roadblock—there are multiple viable ways to implement these systems.

Q: At what point do you think silicon photonics will take over discrete components/modules? I would say silicon photonics has already taken over to a large extent, especially at 100 G and higher data rates. Yield is not something unique to just silicon photonics—it is a critical requirement for any practical technology. However, what silicon photonics uniquely enables is particularly important for the industry. First, it allows companies without their own foundry, like Marvell, to design and manufacture highly complex photonic components by leveraging the existing semiconductor infrastructure. Second, it provides true scalability. Marvell, for example, has been one of the well-known companies implementing silicon photonics for applications between datacenters. Intel pioneered silicon photonics for applications inside the datacenters. Today, many of the large transceiver companies have mostly transitioned to silicon photonics. Given this level of adoption, it is fair to say that silicon photonics has already ‘taken over’ in many key segments of the optical interconnect market. Q: Many silicon photonics systems still rely on external laser packaging. Do you see integrated light sources becoming mainstream, or will off-chip lasers dominate for practical reasons? If you look at the integrated laser work published over the years, including our early demonstrations, Intel’s results, and developments at Infinera (now Nokia), it is clear that integrating light sources heterogeneously on silicon or monolithically in InP is commercially viable. The real obstacle is not technical capability, but perception—many people still assume that integrated lasers will suffer from reliability issues. One concern stems from the behavior of indium phosphide lasers at elevated temperatures. It is true that performance can degrade at high temperatures, but when operated around a base temperature of roughly 70–75 °C, these lasers can already operate perfectly well. In fact, Intel has demonstrated strong reliability in this regime. The key is careful design. Temperature variations can be significant, and the resulting wavelength shifts must be properly managed. In practice, engineers have already developed effective strategies to handle these challenges. So, from a technical perspective, I do not see major roadblocks. The bigger challenge may be changing people’s mindset. Let me also make a prediction. Cooling large-scale server infrastructure is becoming increasingly challenging. The industry is already transitioning from air cooling to liquid cooling, and some are exploring immersion cooling. These approaches can provide more stable and potentially lower operating temperatures, which could create an opportunity for integrated lasers as well. Q: How do you see the potential of heterogeneous integration? I think silicon photonics, as a platform for integration, has enormous potential. What we’ve achieved so far is only scratching the surface. There are multiple paths forward here. You can integrate different materials, combine multiple types of devices within the same material platform, or even move toward stacking multiple device layers. All of these approaches are actively being explored, and I believe many of them will enter production in the near future. As data rates continue to increase, heterogeneous integration will also involve incorporating new higher performance materials, opening up more possibilities. At the same time, integration itself is becoming more complex. We’re moving beyond traditional 2D integration. People are now talking about 2.5D, 3D, even 3.5D integration, which will create many new opportunities for system design and performance improvement. Q: You were one of the early voices pushing co-packaged optics (CPO) for AI systems, now more and more companies are doing this. What convinced you CPO was not just a research direction, but something the industry would truly need? The reason I have advocated for CPO stems from my firm belief that heterogeneous integration is the key to high-speed applications. During my time at Inphi (now at Marvell), we were already seeing the limits of traditional approaches. As data rates continued to increase, techniques like wire bonds simply could not keep up. The logical next step was to bring electronics and photonics closer through heterogeneous integration. That was the original motivation—overcoming speed and bandwidth bottlenecks. But as we developed these approaches, it became clear that they also align very well with what CPO is trying to achieve. Over time, the value of co-packaging has expanded beyond just pushing the highest data rates. We now see it being applied even at relatively lower data rates, especially in large-scale AI systems. Whether you look at scale-up, scale-out, or scale-across systems, they all benefit from tightly integrated photonics with electronics. So, in a way, heterogeneous integration is the essence, and CPO is one of its most important applications. Q: Speed is the reason that drives us from ‘pluggable’ to ‘on board’. Many believe ‘pluggable’ can be extended with better DSPs and packaging, while others argue they won’t scale forever’. From your perspective, will this ‘extension strategy’ scale indefinitely? If you had to point to a single limiting factor, what would ultimately block ‘pluggable’ from keeping up with 100 T, or 200 T switch ASICs? In my opinion, with 100 T or 200 T switches and systems at the horizon, you will need co-packaging. It’s true that the industry is very comfortable with ‘pluggable’ solutions, and there is a strong incentive to extend their life as much as possible. In many scale-up systems, copper will continue to be used as long as it makes sense from a power and cost perspective. For the same reasons, data center operators would naturally prefer to stay with pluggable solutions. However, when developing new products, you have to look ahead. Since late last year, analysts have begun including CPO in their revenue projections, which signals a real shift in the industry. In fact, data center operators and hyperscalers are now actively funding CPO developments. That tells you this is no longer just a research direction. Technically, pluggables can be pushed further, even beyond 200 G per lane, but the cost is power. As you scale bandwidth, the power required for high-speed electrical interfaces and DSPs becomes increasingly difficult to manage. This is where CPO becomes very attractive, especially for scale-up architecture like GPU or accelerator interconnects. I actually think the first major deployment for CPO will happen in scale-up systems, and then expand into scale-out. We’re already seeing strong signals from the ecosystem. Nvidia has demonstrated multiple generations of CPO platforms, including at GTC and ISSCC. From a foundry perspective, this aligns very well with TSMC’s roadmap. The idea of tightly coupling electrical compute with optical I/O is no longer a dream—it could be just a year or two away. That said, predicting the exact timing is always difficult. It’s much easier to explain the past than to forecast the future. When you work at the intersection of technology development and product deployment, there is always uncertainty about when the market will be ready. In many ways, developing new technologies can feel like placing a bet—and that’s simply part of innovation. Q: People often say that co-packaging is always the difficult part, what do you think about that? That perception largely comes from how optical packaging used to be done. If you compare it to electrical packaging, the difference is quite clear. Electrical packaging has been highly automated for a long time. But if you look at the optical packaging in early days, you’ll find yourself doing things one at a time, wire bonding electronics and manually aligning fibers, which are slow and not scalable. But once volume kicks in, things start to change. A lot of these processes are now highly automated. There are still operators involved, but the work is largely handled by automatic equipment. Efficient fiber coupling is still a challenge, no question. But there are already solutions out there, and they’re getting better. As volume continues to ramp, I believe the fiber coupling part will gradually be automated as well. If you look at where we are today, photonics manufacturing is already very different from five years ago, and nothing like ten years ago. And honestly, things are only going to move faster from here. Q: Are Through-Silicon Via (TSVs) essential for CPO? We demonstrated and published extensively on TSV as an option way to integrate electronics on top and then route down into silicon photonics bottom layer. But you don’t have to always do that. You can flip the architecture and put the optics on top instead, and some groups are actively exploring that. One reason people hesitate is thermal. Electronics tend to run hotter than optics, so it’s often easier to heat-sink from the top, if the electronics are there. But that’s not a fundamental limitation—there are many alternative thermal management strategies people are exploring as well. There are also practical considerations in the fab that may lead to a preference for one configuration over another. Early on, there was a strong tendency to adopt TSV-based approach. For example, you now have packaging techniques like fan-out wafer-level packaging (FOWLP), which does not require TSVs at all. Several companies are already offering this technology, and some are even beginning to deploy CPO based on it. So, the absence of TSVs is not a fundamental roadblock—there are multiple viable ways to implement these systems. I've started paying serious attention to AI photonics.

The future competition for GPUs may become about "who can transmit data faster."

$AAOI $LITE

Space Chips + Optics
• $LITE - optical networking
• $COHR - lasers + photonics
• $AAOI - optical transceivers
• $QRVO - RF chips
• $ADI - analog systems
• $AVGO - networking infrastructure
• $NVDA - AI + compute backbone
Critical Resources
• $MP - rare earths
• $FCX-copp

text:
Mine for sure! 😉 He didn’t get into photonics until way later (I got $axti at $16). But he did better on some stocks (I sadly sold a lot of my $nbis even though I love that company, as I moved funds to some other stocks). And he has a lower DCA on a few.
But I have a lot more exposure to space 

I just started really focusing on investing last year in October. I have a lot more flexibility since I have my own business and he works pretty intense hours, so that helps me do a lot more research. But we’re not competitive with investing (just most other things!). We always celebrate each other’s wins

user_name: Sima Acan, MP for Oakville West
text:
In 1976 Taiwan sent 20 engineers abroad to learn chip-making from scratch. Decades later they control our global semiconductor supply chain. 

Today at committee, I asked: What can Canada learn from this?

We have our own unique Canadian Photonics Fabrication Centre, the talent, the resources, and the moment. The opportunity is now.

#ParliamentHill #HouseofCommons #OakvilleWest

text:
People still aren't bullish enough on the companies turning AI from prototype to deployed infrastructure.

The real moat isn't just the flashy model it's the lithography, photonics, and orbital comms that make scaling possible without melting the grid.

Names like $EUV (lithography/photonics), $DIPR (space/satcom), and $XA (cyber layer) sit at those chokepoints.

Early to Emerging stage. Market hasn't fully priced the physical world bottlenecks yet.

text:
Real reason why $NVDA earnings matter so much tonight.

Not just the headline beat/miss.

It’s the forward commentary on:

• Rubin ramp
• Blackwell margins
• Quantum-X deployments
• Spectrum-X timing
• CPO / silicon photonics adoption
• HBM pricing & packaging constraints

Because Nvidia is the operating system of the entire AI infrastructure stack.

The biggest risk into earnings, in my opinion:

Margins.

If Rubin + GB300 ramps accelerate while HBM4 and advanced packaging costs stay elevated, the market may finally start questioning whether AI infra growth can continue without margin compression.

But if Nvidia guides cleanly above expectations again?

Then the AI capex supercycle is still alive — and semis probably squeeze even harder into H2.

It’s a macro event for the entire AI economy.

text:
$SIVE (Sivers Photonics) is my highest-conviction pick for the strongest stock appreciation in the entire photonics sector over the next 2–3 years.
Current price: ~45 SEK. Long-term target (2027–2028): 150–250 SEK (3–5x upside potential).

Sivers is a pure-play leader in high-performance CW DFB laser arrays; the critical light source enabling co-packaged optics (CPO) and high-speed pluggable modules for AI data centers.

Evidence and data backing the thesis:

AI interconnect demand explosion: As clusters scale to 800G/1.6T+, copper hits hard limits on heat, signal loss, and density. Optical interconnects are becoming mandatory, driving massive demand for lasers.

Strong ecosystem positioning: Partnerships and design wins with Ayar Labs (SuperNova), POET Technologies, Marvell Celestial, Jabil, and others. Recent CHIPS Act grant with BAE Systems and MIT Lincoln Lab adds U.S. government validation and non-dilutive funding.

Dual growth drivers: Primary: AI photonics. Secondary: defense, LiDAR, and satellite comms. This diversification reduces risk while the AI ramp accelerates.

Small-cap asymmetry: Low current revenue base means successful volume ramps can drive explosive growth. 

Analysts see significant revenue scaling as major CPO programs move into production in 2027–2028.

Qualitative edge: Sivers has spent years perfecting high-reliability lasers with the performance needed for AI-scale deployments. 

The combination of government backing, strong customer pipeline, and being European (reducing geopolitical risk) gives it a real moat in a market where supply chain security matters more every day.

Risks:
Execution on volume ramp and yield
Potential dilution if more capital is needed
Competition in a fast-moving space

$SIVE sits right in the middle of the laser chokepoint for the next wave of AI interconnects. The photonics transition is structural and inevitable; Sivers is one of the best-positioned small-caps to benefit with real customer traction and government support.

This one ages very well. Worth accumulating on weakness.

text:
laser / photonics sector looks like one of the highest-conviction 6-month setups in the entire ai stack right now.

copper is hitting its physical limits fast as clusters scale to 800g and 1.6t+. heat, signal loss, and density issues are forcing hyperscalers to switch to optical interconnects at rack and campus scale. 

the data backs it up hard:
1.6t optical modules are moving from lab to commercial deployment in 2026

optical content per gpu is expected to rise 1.3–1.5x as scale-out and scale-across networks expand

silicon photonics market is forecasted to grow from ~$2.86b in 2025 to $28.75b by 2034 (29%+ cagr), with the biggest ramp hitting in the next 2–3 years

names like $LITE, $SIVE, $COHR, and $GLW sit right in the middle of the laser and optical engine supply chain with real design wins and qualification momentum. 

the ones that can deliver high-volume, high-reliability lasers and transceivers are going to see strong re-rating as hyperscalers pull harder.

the short-term risk is execution and any delay in volume ramps, but the structural demand is undeniable. 
this is not narrative; it’s the next physical bottleneck after memory and power.

the laser/photonics sector has one of the cleanest asymmetric setup for the next 6 months in my book.

text:
$LITE’s OCS tech being called an “overnight success” after 10 years of R&D (with heavy involvement from $SLXN and $P4O)  is exactly how the best photonics plays look right now.

the CEO’s answer on Chinese competitors was sharp; they’ve been iterating for a decade while others are still catching up. high-powered lasers originally developed for undersea applications are now being repurposed for co-packaged optics, which is the direction hyperscalers are heading as clusters scale to 800G/1.6T+.

i’m biased that this is the kind of deep technical moat that actually matters in the interconnect layer. 

hyperscalers are hitting real limits on heat, signal loss, and density that copper can’t solve. the names that have spent years perfecting lasers and optical circuit switches keep the structural edge.

the comments asking about other long-term OCS players like Huber-Suhner ($HUBN) acquiring Polatis in 2016 are right to look for similar multi-year development stories. 

the ones calling it “mandatory reading” get that this is not hype; it’s real manufacturing and qualification work that takes a decade.

this cycle rewards the execution plays solving proven physical bottlenecks that hyperscalers can’t pause for. 

photonics and OCS are one of those constraints right now, and $LITE (with the ecosystem around it) sits right in the middle. the setup has legs. this one ages well.

text:
最近光模块很热，尤其是DSP，CPO等相关的新一代“光芯片”技术，引领整个光产业的股票狂飙

YTD 表现里 AAOI +441%、LITE +166%、COHR +97%——同一个板块，分化 4 倍

而接下来 12-24 个月真正定生死的是 CPO 时间表：

2026 H2：NVDA 的 Quantum-X / Spectrum-X Photonics 首批 scale-out CPO switch 量产
2027 H2：Rubin Ultra 整机 scale-up CPO 首批出货

这两个节点 TAM 完全不在一个量级。按 Lumentum 自己披露的数据，Rubin Ultra 架构里 scale-up:scale-out 光引擎比是 648:144，约 4.5:1。意思是市场现在 price in 的是 2026 H2 的 scale-out 故事，但真正的钱在 2027 H2 之后才开始进来——这是一年的时间错位。

这个错位会改变一件事的发生：现在追 AAOI 这种 capacity arbitrage 的标的，赚的是 2025-2026 的供应紧张钱；2027 之后真正的赢家是有结构性 IP moat 的——LITE 的 EML 激光器（全球市占 50-60%）和 MRVL 的 1.6T Ara DSP 平台（已经是行业事实标准）。传言 NVDA 已经在跟 Lumentum 要 2027 年 5000 万颗激光器产能，是当前 trajectory 的两倍。

视角站的更高来看——光模块本质上不是独立行情，是 NVDA $90B 资本部署的下游放大版：

COHR：NVDA 投 $2B + 5 个 CPO 产品线合作
LITE：NVDA 投 $2B + 多年采购承诺 + EML 激光器锁定
MRVL：NVDA 投 $2B + NVLink Fusion，optical FY27 guide +50% YoY
Corning：$6B Meta deal + 多年协议

NVDA 这套操作不是单点投资，是把整条光互联供应链（激光器 → DSP → transceiver → CPO 引擎 → 光纤）整体锁死。AMD、Google、AWS 想做对等的 rack-level 出货，光是补齐这条供应链就要 3-5 年。

操作上分层我是这么看的：

Tier 1（结构性持仓）：MRVL、COHR——NVDA 资本绑定 + 技术 moat 长，按住不动
Tier 2（NVDA capex 高 beta）：LITE、CRDO——单一产品强但暴露窄，单仓别超 5%
Tier 3（capacity 套利不是 moat 套利）：AAOI、FN、ALAB——估值已极端，等任何 10%+ 回调再说

最后一个最容易被忽略的点：光模块板块不是 NVDA 的对冲，是 NVDA 的杠杆。NVDA 涨 10%，光板涨 15-20%；NVDA 跌 10%，光板跌 20-30%。beta 1.5-2x 是双向的。

所以仓位上要意识到 NVDA + 光模块合计就是同一个押注，别超过 AI infra 配置的 70%。剩下 30% 应该留给 power（GEV、VRT）、cooling（MOD）、custom silicon（AVGO）这些和 NVDA 资本绑定较少的子赛道——这样组合内部才有真正的对冲。

4.5:1。意思是市场现在 price in 的是 2026 H2 的 scale-out 故事，但真正的钱在 2027 H2 之后才开始进来——这是一年的时间错位。 这个错位会改变一件事的发生：现在追 AAOI 这种 capacity arbitrage 的标的，赚的是 2025-2026 的供应紧张钱；2027 之后真正的赢家是有结构性 IP moat 的——LITE 的 EML 激光器（全球市占 50-60%）和 MRVL 的 1.6T Ara DSP 平台（已经是行业事实标准）。传言 NVDA 已经在跟 Lumentum 要 2027 年 5000 万颗激光器产能，是当前 trajectory 的两倍。 视角站的更高来看——光模块本质上不是独立行情，是 NVDA $90B 资本部署的下游放大版： COHR：NVDA 投 $2B + 5 个 CPO 产品线合作 LITE：NVDA 投 $2B + 多年采购承诺 + EML 激光器锁定 MRVL：NVDA 投 $2B + NVLink Fusion，optical FY27 guide +50% YoY Corning：$6B Meta deal + 多年协议 NVDA 这套操作不是单点投资，是把整条光互联供应链（激光器 → DSP → transceiver → CPO 引擎 → 光纤）整体锁死。AMD、Google、AWS 想做对等的 rack-level 出货，光是补齐这条供应链就要 3-5 年。 操作上分层我是这么看的： Tier 1（结构性持仓）：MRVL、COHR——NVDA 资本绑定 + 技术 moat 长，按住不动 Tier 2（NVDA capex 高 beta）：LITE、CRDO——单一产品强但暴露窄，单仓别超 5% Tier 3（capacity 套利不是 moat 套利）：AAOI、FN、ALAB——估值已极端，等任何 10%+ 回调再说 最后一个最容易被忽略的点：光模块板块不是 NVDA 的对冲，是 NVDA 的杠杆。NVDA 涨 10%，光板涨 15-20%；NVDA 跌 10%，光板跌 20-30%。beta 1.5-2x 是双向的。 所以仓位上要意识到 NVDA + 光模块合计就是同一个押注，别超过 AI infra 配置的 70%。剩下 30% 应该留给 power（GEV、VRT）、cooling（MOD）、custom silicon（AVGO）这些和 NVDA 资本绑定较少的子赛道——这样组合内部才有真正的对冲。 #NVDA #光模块 #AIinfra #CPO"}}}, "rest_id": "2057145606884524131", "source": "Twitter Web App", Japan has the parts of the stack that sound too boring (until the shipment is late) 4062.T Ibiden: the substrate floor under high-end AI packages. 2802.T Ajinomoto: the soup and MSG company behind Ajinomoto Build-up Film, the insulating film inside those substrates. 4063.T Shin-Etsu: wafers and materials before the chip is even a chip. 8035.T Tokyo Electron: process tools that make advanced manufacturing repeatable. 5332.T TOTO: ceramics. 3110.T Nittobo: glass fiber. 4004.T Resonac: specialty materials and packaging-adjacent chemistry. JSR matters too, but it is not a clean public ticker anymore after the 2024 delisting. That almost makes the point better imo. Some of the most important AI infrastructure is already disappearing from the easy screen. pattern is simple: AI looks American at the model layer. looks Taiwanese at the foundry layer. looks Korean at the memory layer. text: I’ve been early on the photonics trade. I’m not questioning the trend or the technology, I question why a small Swedish company with literally ZERO proven track record in both order deliveries and manufacturing expansion and annual revenues in $30-40M and neg earnings should be valued at $3B. Competition in this field is fierce and will get even rougher. Size matters and a large player in this field should motivate a premium share price - NOT the other way around. That’s all.

text:
The AI infrastructure trade is beginning to rotate away from crowded GPU beta and into the hidden process control layers enabling advanced packaging and silicon photonics. 

$6830.TW is increasingly being reframed from a niche semiconductor analysis service company into a potential inspection and metrology proxy tied to CoWoS, InFO and optical interconnect scaling. 

As @rtk17025 points out, MSSCORPS may be one of Taiwan’s few domestic suppliers with exposure to high precision AOI and laser repair systems, giving it potential leverage to wafer defect inspection, photomasks, advanced packaging and silicon photonics. 

The key debate now is whether the company can successfully transition from a technical services model into a higher value “service + equipment + licensing” platform tied to Taiwan, Japan and U.S. silicon photonics IP.

text:
Solid take, Hunter...  $ALMU is flying under the radar. 15+ years in semis and interconnects here: GPUs steal the show, but moving massive data at scale is the real bottleneck — power, heat, and copper limits are killing efficiency. 

Aeluma’s III-V-on-silicon photonics on standard 200/300mm wafers could slash costs and scale like crazy vs. old InP tech. Strong partners (Tower, AIM, gov contracts) and real tech edge.

Early revenue, high execution risk, but at ~$414M cap, the upside in the AI optics wave is huge. 

Watch this one.Great thread. Adding to the list.(DYOR — small caps = high risk!)

text:
A team at Westlake University in China just published a handheld cancer detector in Nature Photonics. Single drop of blood, 94.9% accuracy for early-stage lung cancer, 10,000× more sensitive than the standard lab test.

Chip cost: $5.Worth paying attention to, but also worth reading carefully. That 94.9% comes from 171 patient samples. The device replaces refrigerator-sized lab equipment with an LED, a photodetector, and a 3D metamaterial chip. Genuinely impressive physics.

However, the gap nobody's talking about: every year produces 3-4 "revolutionary cancer detection" papers. Almost none survive the jump from Nature paper to clinical validation at scale.

Caveat here: 171 patients is a starting point, not proof. But the engineering approach is what's worth watching.

text: Massive gains often trigger regulatory scrutiny—expected given the timing of the Nasdaq dual-listing news. A "lottery ticket" position is one thing, but chasing this at 30x is a different game entirely.

text:
I just hit my first 10-bagger (+943%) on $SIVE in under 3 months! Discovered via @aleabitoreddit , a fundamental deep dive showed massive undervaluation in the photonics sector.

I initially bought 900 shares at 8.2 SEK, but the stock took off so fast I only managed to add a bit more below 20 SEK. The biggest lesson this investment taught me: trust your analysis and build a heavier position sooner when the conviction is there.

I locked in profits by trimming 1/3 at 49 SEK. With the price now around 85.55 SEK, I won't be adding at current valuations, but I am happily letting the rest ride as long as the company keeps delivering. Great investment, invaluable lessons.

text:
The market has figured out photonics. It’s catching on to power semis. But It still hasn’t woken up to fiber. 

The real chokepoint is sitting in the dirt. I'm positioned accordingly in $HLIT.

“Global fiber prices have risen ~70% from a 2021 trough of $3.70 to ~$6.30 per fiber-kilometer.”
 — Tom’s Hardware

“North American demand growth is projected at 22% to 25% this year, while supply expansion trails at 12% to 19%.”  
— Rebio Group estimates, reported by Tom’s Hardware

“That dual demand [AI + BEAD] elevates our fiber forecast, but it also makes the shortage more pronounced.”  
— Anis Khemakhem, Clearfield

“AI-focused data centers require significantly more fiber — about 36x more fiber than traditional CPU-based racks — to handle the massive data volumes and high-speed connectivity required by GPU clusters.”  
— Rahul Puri, CEO Optical Networking, STL

“Data center fiber demand grew roughly 76% year-on-year in 2025… the segment is projected to account for 30% of total global fiber demand by 2027. In 2024, that figure was below 5%.”
 — CRU data, reported by Tom’s Hardware

“AI has pushed fiber demand to levels we’ve never seen to ensure the incredible speeds inside data centers and between campuses. The many, massive data center builds we’re seeing today are driving huge volumes of intra-bay, inter-bay and middle-mile fiber.”
 — Anis Khemakhem, CCO, Clearfield

“The surge in hyperscale and AI network loads has significantly increased our expectations for fiber demand. Enterprise sales grew 58% year-over-year in Q3 2025, driven by continued strong adoption of Corning’s Gen AI products.”
 — John McGirr, Corning

“The U.S. will need to add 213.3 million more fiber miles by 2029, more than doubling its current amount from 159.6 million fiber miles to 372.9 million miles.”
 — Rahul Puri, STL

Major beneficiaries include:
$HLIT, $NOK, $LUMN, $CXDO, $CLFD, $BDC, $GLW

If AST SpaceMobile is the company building the connectivity layer from space, $HLIT (Harmonic) is its terrestrial mirror. The platform layer powers every fiber and cable network on the ground. Companies that build real technical moats inside secular spending waves, and get ignored while doing it, don’t stay ignored forever. AST taught me what that looks like before the re-rate. I’m seeing it again with Harmonic. And the timing matters. We’re at the front end of a global fiber supercycle, operators everywhere are being forced to densify, upgrade to DOCSIS 4.0, and push fiber deeper into their networks simultaneously. That spending wave is just starting to inflect, and Harmonic cOS is the platform sitting at the center of it. Companies that build real technical moats inside secular spending waves, and get ignored while doing it, don’t stay ignored forever. AST taught me what that looks like before the re-rate. I’m seeing it again with Harmonic. The operator accumulation flywheel is accelerating for Harmonic. Rest-of-market revenue surged 78% YoY in Q1, now 42% of total, up from near-zero a few years ago. New wins are landing every quarter across regional ISPs, international carriers, and fiber-first builders. This is the same pattern AST followed: anchor with tier-1 partners, prove the platform, then watch adoption compound as word travels through the operator community. Harmonic engineered a defined platform that creates a true moat legacy hardware vendors simply cannot replicate: • cOS™ — the industry’s first virtualized CMTS, replacing expensive proprietary hardware boxes with software running on generic servers • Unified DOCSIS + Fiber — a single stack managing both cable and fiber subscribers, the only platform that does this at scale • 150+ operators, 45.7M devices deployed — deep operational stickiness in billing, provisioning, and field workflows • 75% power reduction across seven successive hardware generations, iterative engineering lead nearly a decade in the making • AI-driven network intelligence, an entirely new growth vector built on top of the virtualized core This is full-stack vertical integration done right. The result? A company shedding its legacy video business ($145M divestiture closing Q2 2026) to emerge as a pure-play broadband software intelligence platform. Q1 2026 broadband revenue surged 43% YoY. Rest-of-market revenue, the diversification engine, grew 78%. Record $582M backlog, up 87% YoY. Management raised full-year guidance to $475–495M. EPS crushed estimates ($0.17 vs $0.10). In an environment where every ISP is racing to upgrade to DOCSIS 4.0 and deploy fiber, a platform that manages both from one stack is becoming table stakes, and $HLIT already has it. I’m positioned accordingly."}}}, "rest_id": "2058901649016627396", "source": "Twitter Web Client", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "112995", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2058975546714046804", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "39235", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Jensen Huang is back in Taiwan for $NVDA GTC Taipei discussing next-gen AI infrastructure, photonics, agentic AI, and supply chain coordination with $TSM.

Two years after bears called the top over a viral moment, Nvidia is now nearing a $5.5T market cap while $QQQ is roughly 50% higher.

The AI buildout also continues expanding beyond GPUs into CPU and orchestration layers tied to $ARM, $QCOM, and $INTC.

text:
PHOTONICS 101: How AI data moves at the speed of light.

Every prompt sent to ChatGPT travels through five layers of photonic infrastructure before a single token returns.

Why photons beat electrons:
Copper electrons hit a thermal wall above 800G. Photons don't.
Light doesn't heat the medium. 80% less power. The only path forward at 1.6T+.

The stack, from raw wafer to AI datacenter:

01 · Foundation — Wafers and photonic foundries. Where light meets silicon.
$TSM, $GFS, $ASML

02 · Components — Lasers, modulators, detectors. The active parts that generate, encode, and read light.
$MRVL, $POET, $SIVE

03 · Modules — Optical transceivers and light engines. Convert electrical to optical. The CPO transition lives here.
$LITE, $COHR, $AAOI, $JBL

04 · Systems — Switches and networking gear. Route optical traffic between racks. Where 800G is becoming 1.6T right now.
$ANET, $CIEN, $CSCO

05 · Deployment — AI datacenters and hyperscalers. $725B in 2026 capex. Every GPU rack runs on optical interconnects.
$NVDA, $MSFT, $GOOGL, $META

Every layer wins together. Hyperscalers buy more switches. Switches need more transceivers. Transceivers need more lasers. Lasers need more wafers.

The AI capex flywheel turns the entire stack.

Five layers. One supercycle.

text:
Germans are so technical and precise that certain mistakes are only considered stupidity.

In an MSc lab course, I heard about an international student who was trying to build a laser in the lab as part of a photonics coursework. This student was not wearing safety goggles but (s)he was supposed to. (S)he looked directly into the laser beam and got injured — got blind. To be honest, I only heard the story from my photonics professor, so I have no idea whether the student was compensated by insurance in Germany.

During a laser safety briefing, my photonics professor shared the story and said that it was not an accident. He used a German term which technically means, “you stupidly injured yourself.”

I asked why, and the professor responded: “If you know something is going to hurt you and you go ahead and do it anyway, then get hurt, that’s not an accident — that’s stupidity. And there’s a German word for that.”

Germany doesn’t have time for your stupidity! Beware⚠️
