text:
Before turning to photonics, I tried my luck in biotech. The field caught my interest quite strongly, and for about two years I read almost exclusively books in that area. Overall, I had a fairly good track record.

I don’t own any of the four stocks $QURE, $CMPS, $CAPR, or https://t.co/zgqEYAzfFg, anymore. Back then, all four were asymmetric bets for me, and contrary to the odds, three of them worked out very well (multibaggers), while even the fourth still ended up slightly positive.

A few weeks ago, I made another biotech investment where I see an asymmetric opportunity. Unfortunately, I can’t share it here, even though I’ve written a full write-up on it. The company is simply too small, and sharing it would likely drive the price up significantly. Ideally, it should re-rate for fundamental reasons, not because of attention or momentum. In cases like this, a paid Substack model would actually make sense.

Once the case plays out, either successfully or not, I’ll share it here. That way it will be documented whether my “biotech touch” is still intact.

Many people probably associate me primarily with photonics, but in reality I’m more of a generalist covering a range of different fields. I try to avoid having only a single hammer in the toolbox, otherwise every problem starts looking like a nail.
embedded_text: Bought $QURE last week. The FDA must be crazy not to approve their candidate for Huntington's disease. Together with $CAPR, $CMPS and $CMP.AX, these are four companies with very promising data that are very likely to receive approval within the next 1-2 years.

but I wanted to write something up because of the pictured quote inspired me * When I first invested in $LPTH over a year ago, my initial thesis was largely driven by geopolitical tailwinds, especially China’s germanium export restrictions and the implications for domestic supply chains. But the reason I’ve continued to hold and add more shares very quickly evolved. And one aspect that truly strengthened my conviction in my investment is the leadership of @skrubin. Under his direction, the company has very clearly positioned itself at the forefront of EO/IR systems, threat detection, and advanced optics... markets with durable, long-term demand driven by national security and technological acceleration. And this current company positioning is not solely a case of “preparation meeting opportunity” with BlackDiamond serving as an alternative to germanium at the right moment. While that was an important inflection point no doubt, what gives me greater confidence going forward now is how Sam is actively compounding that advantage. He’s iterating on the core technology with next-generation BDNL materials, expanding applications into air defense and space-based systems, strengthening relationships with prime contractors like $LMT, $LHX, $ESLT and Anduril, pursuing strategic M&A (G5 and AMI), and methodically moving the company further up the value chain. These distinctions truly matter as some leaders benefit from favorable macro conditions while others build enduring platforms on top of them. Sam is clearly doing the latter. He’s turning a geopolitical tailwind into a structural competitive advantage and that’s what sets up LightPath not just to participate in this current cycle, but to compound through it. I truly think that the quote “The secret to investing is to invest in unstoppable people building inevitable things” genuinely applies to @skrubin and it clearly comes across with every investor engagement he participates in and not because of any sort of hype, but because of his clarity, his strategic intent, and consistent execution. He’s one of the few CEOs whose every single interview, earnings call, and even social media update I genuinely look forward to as an investor. Leaders like Sam are how companies transform and flourish, and I truly believe that over the next 5–10 years, LightPath has the potential to become the next FLIR Systems (now part Teledyne) or Leonardo DRS."}}}, "rest_id": "2025957796156510217", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "26120", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2057884513276240309", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2956", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

It has now been two months since I published my first deep dive on $SIVE 

The stock is up 10x since then and, honestly, I think it is now massively overextended.

SEK 21.7B market cap on SEK 306M of revenue.
More than 70x sales.

I remain bullish on the long-term photonics / AI connectivity thesis, and I was probably one of the first public bulls talking about the company. But even then, the current valuation is absolutely astronomical.

At this point, the market is already pricing in a brutal revenue ramp, massive conversion from NRE into product revenue, and near-perfect execution over the coming years.

That said, I’m not fucking selling

$COHR, $MTSI, $STM and Hamamatsu quietly dominating SpaceX’s lasercomm supply chain is another real-world proof point that photonics demand is expanding beyond terrestrial data centers.

the post maps the space-qualified chain perfectly; precision optics/coatings/EDFAs, high-speed drivers/TIAs, PAT control ASICs, and high-sensitivity detectors. 

flight heritage is the ultimate moat in this market, and these four are already winning the first generation.

this is bullish for the broader photonics layer. 

hyperscalers need reliable low-latency global connectivity for AI data centers, edge computing, and the full physical stack. starlink-type orbital networks solve real bottlenecks that terrestrial grids can’t fix fast enough. the same laser and optical engine technology that powers inter-satellite links has direct read-through to terrestrial CPO and pluggable ramps.

this cycle rewards the execution plays solving proven physical bottlenecks that hyperscalers literally can’t pause for. photonics (lasers, optics, detectors) is one of those constraints, and the space demand layer just adds another structural tailwind on top of the terrestrial AI buildout.

the setup has legs. this one ages well.

text:
This is the old debate: should I invest in an enabler or beneficiary?

Meaning: 

Does this company aid the AI buildout, or just use AI to improve its service? 

Examples of enablers are semis, memory, neoclouds, photonics. The picks and shovels. Beneficiaries are fintech, SaaS, healthcare, etc..

Right now, enablers are gaining all of the value.

$SIVE is up 1000% in 2 moths.
$MU is up 200% in 2 months.

Yet the beneficiaries are losing their value.

$ZETA is down 50% in 2 years.
$DUOL is down 80% in 1 year.

What's happening to the beneficiaries? They're going down because investors think AI will replace their business.

Every investor is screaming "SaaS is dead."

But that's really only for the vibe coded SaaS companies, not the incumbents.

It seems to be the case that switching costs are too high and this gives them time to add all the features that your favorite startup flexes on Twitter.

That is why so many of these companies in the beneficiary niche are undervalued.

Also, let's not forget, with AI, it actually allows these companies to produce more with less cost -> actually benefiting them overall.

Not a bad place to be buying these names for the long-term imo:

$WDAY (Workday inc)
$NOW (ServiceNow inc)
$SAP (Sap IE)
$CRM (Salesforce inc)
$TEAM (Atlassian Corp)
$DUOL (Duolingo inc) 
$ZETA (Zeta Global)
$INTU (Intuit inc)
$VEEV (Veeva Systems inc)

And more.

Also, I'm not saying enablers will go down.

I'm invested in a lot of them.

I'm just saying the hate on beneficiaries is not deserved.

Yes, some will die, but most of them with moats, will continue to push forward.

And actually, produce more efficiently in the future, all while it is at a discounted price today.

This is just my 2c.

- Con

Glass subs replace them w/no bend or warp.

The LIDE patent portfolio is dense and covers the core process (Laser-Induced Deep Etching) for forming Through-Glass Vias (TGVs) without micro-cracking. This matters because glass is so brittle that conventional drilling (mechanical or standard laser ablation) creates tiny fault lines. When the chip heats up, those fault lines expand and shatter the substrate. LIDE solves this at the physics level. Competitor check: Canon has TGV tools but uses a different process. JNTC (Korea) has proprietary in-house tech but explicitly avoids LPKF-style equipment. Corning has laser capabilities but is a glass maker, not a tool vendor. No other company has a production-proven LIDE equivalent. Customer lock-in: CEO Klaus Fiedler stated that more than 80% of major global semiconductor customers have selected LPKF equipment for test/R&D environments. Re-qualification of a different TGV formation method would take 1-2 years and require re-running all reliability testing. Toll booth confirmation: An Intel patent (granted 2025) explicitly describes LIDE-formed TGVs being restructured for optical waveguides in a CPO architecture. This is the definition of being designed into the process flow. Widening moat: LPKF is expanding beyond TGV formation into glass-package singulation, multilayer glass-stack laser bonding, and CPO-on-glass integration. They are moving from one tool in the process to potentially three or four. We are so early. text:
$COHR 

There are many enticing photonics stocks out there to invest in. For me personally, Coherent stands out as the leading full-stack powerhouse that controls the critical AI-driven photonics supply chain from wafer to module. The company controls every layer in house via deep vertical integration spanning engineered materials, InP/GaAs laser chips, silicon photonics platforms, VCSEL arrays, and precision optics. This is extremely advantageous in a supply-constrained world. 

Nvidia’s $2B strategic investment and multi-year supply deal cement Coherent’s position as the go-to enabler for hyperscaler AI racks, next-gen co-packaged optics (CPO), and optical circuit switching that’s powering the entire AI infrastructure buildout and the explosive growth of GPU clusters worldwide.

Fundamentally, Coherent is a high conviction play where the current ~$70B valuation still looks undervalued given the explosive AI tailwinds and durable moat. That moat comes from true end-to-end ownership few can match, creating high switching costs, superior economics, and supply certainty hyperscalers crave for. Revenue and margins are steadily climbing. Backlog extends into 2028 with 20%+ forward growth, analysts keep hiking targets to $400+ on strong buy consensus, yet the street hasn’t fully priced in the long-term dominance by the end of the decade. I see this compounding into a potentially a $200B + MC beast as the company owns the physical layer of the AI supercycle. 

In conclusion, Coherent shall be the most stable photonics play still w/ highly promising upside for years to come.

For me personally, Coherent stands out as the leading full-stack powerhouse that controls the critical AI-driven photonics supply chain from wafer to module. The company controls every layer in house via deep vertical integration spanning engineered materials, InP/GaAs laser chips, silicon photonics platforms, VCSEL arrays, and precision optics. This is extremely advantageous in a supply-constrained world. Nvidia’s $2B strategic investment and multi-year supply deal cement Coherent’s position as the go-to enabler for hyperscaler AI racks, next-gen co-packaged optics (CPO), and optical circuit switching that’s powering the entire AI infrastructure buildout and the explosive growth of GPU clusters worldwide. Fundamentally, Coherent is a high conviction play where the current ~$70B valuation still looks undervalued given the explosive AI tailwinds and durable moat. That moat comes from true end-to-end ownership few can match, creating high switching costs, superior economics, and supply certainty hyperscalers crave for. Revenue and margins are steadily climbing. Backlog extends into 2028 with 20%+ forward growth, analysts keep hiking targets to $400+ on strong buy consensus, yet the street hasn’t fully priced in the long-term dominance by the end of the decade. I see this compounding into a potentially a $200B + MC beast as the company owns the physical layer of the AI supercycle.
